question: Describe the heat transfer area and structural characteristics of the steam generator.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale reactor pressurizer, specifying the alloys and standards for each component, such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting. It emphasizes the use of corrosion-resistant materials in accordance with ASME BPVC Section II specifications to ensure compatibility and durability in the reactor coolant system.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the NuScale Power Module’s Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems, including the reactor pressure vessel, steam generators, safety valves, and associated piping and valves. It outlines how these components are integrated within the module, specifies their boundaries between primary and secondary systems, and references where detailed design bases for individual components can be found in other sections of the report.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: Each NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel contains two integral steam generators, which are part of the reactor coolant system and share the vessel’s pressure boundary. The steam generator system includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and internal plenums, with the reactor pressure vessel itself forming the shell and pressure boundary for the steam generators.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

edges.description: Section A provides detailed material and component information about the pressurizer, which is a structural part of the reactor pressure vessel described more generally in Section B's summary of the reactor coolant system.

Section A provides references to material specifications and structural details of the pressurizer, while Section B describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System, both of which are subsystems within the Reactor Coolant System and are structurally connected through their roles in heat transfer and system integrity.

Section A describes the components within the reactor pressure vessel, including the steam generators, while Section B explains the function of the Decay Heat Removal System, which relies on the steam generators for heat transfer during certain events, thus connecting the structural characteristics of the steam generators in A to their operational role in B.
